speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four, Sam Koslowski. You were talking about what you were doing in the first-- when you came back to that city. And then where did you go, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when I came to-- the-- the Polish people started to kill Jews, and I run away to Wroclaw. In Wroclaw, I stayed not-- not too long. And I came to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Regensburg-- I lived in Regensburg for five years, I lived there. And I said, I wanted to go to America. Well, I supposed to go to Israel-- I supposed to go. And I bought a-- for washing machines, to wash clothing, for $18,000. And I bought it there-- I bought a machine to be a-- I want to be a [NON-ENGLISH], to work in Israel. I sold-- I know, later on, he came, somebody came and said, don't go to Israel because there's a lot of suffering there. Because you wouldn't have your own what to live. You wouldn't-- it's no good. Don't go to Israel. And I changed my mind to America. I changed my mind. I came to America. I went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? When did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I came to America maybe 1944, '45-- '45, end of '45. I came to America, and I went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait-- you said that you lived in Germany for four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then was it 1945 or 1950?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1950, right-- you're right. 1950, I came to America. And I went to work. I went to work right away because they was looking for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you and your wife have any children by that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we had Esther-- Esther was one child. We rent an apartment. And I was going to work. I made a-- made nice living. I mean, because I'm a specialist. A specialist, you know mechanic?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mechanic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not every mechanic is a specialist. I'm a specialist. I worked by somebody in the factory. And he gave me-- he make me a big shot. And I work. I worked till I said I didn't like it, the work. I tell you the truth. I didn't like the work. I wanted, for myself. I [INAUDIBLE], it was Pesach. Pesach, 1946-- '46. I went over-- in 1956. [NON-ENGLISH], 1950, I came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1952, '52, '52, I think, '53? I went to-- and I bought the farm. A farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I worked very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of farm was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A chicken farm. You-- you know, what is-- chicken. I had 500 chicken. And I could make a living. [LAUGHS] I used to take the eggs and the truck. Oh, I couldn't drive a-- couldn't drive nothing. Mr. Siegel came, said that he was a-- old sale, from cars. I said, Mr. Siegel, you have to give me a car. I don't have a car, and I can't drive. [LAUGHS] He said, OK. We will learn you. We learn you. I was young. He learned me to drive. And he's salesmen-- which he sold me the station wagon. He taught me how to drive a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I took the eggs, and I sold it house by house, sold eggs. And people saw a refugee-- you know, they're like a big surprise. And they bought the eggs from me, fresh eggs. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish people-- not Polish, I mean Christian. They bought them from me. I became a millionaire. [INAUDIBLE]. Later, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva. And I have my son, Harry. My son is an anti-Semite, you know? You know what means, anti-Semite?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. You were saying you were a millionaire. Tell us how did-- how did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a big job to tell that. I-- my son was married. He lives in Florida. You don't know my son-- he's a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: I gave him so much money to became a doctor. He didn't suffer for-- for money. For example, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you like to know? Yeah? You like to know? No? Well, we really wanted to hear about what your life was like when you came to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to New York. First of all, I went to New Jersey, and I bought the farm. I used to make $5,000 a year extra to save it-- $5,00 a year. And I mean, I had for 10 years. Later, I went to New York, and I bought an apartment house. There's nothing necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, are there any other stories about-- that you can recall now that we didn't talk about in the interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My mother-- when my-- I was with my mother, sitting on the ground with the-- before the German took her away to kill. She said to me, she-- she said, God should help me. One child should survive. And I survived. I still alive. That's what God helped, and I survived. That's something like she said.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what's mean [NON-ENGLISH]? No. She's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how-- you know what's mean [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Righteous. Righteous?

speaker: interviewer
text: She was righteous? Righteous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Righteous?

speaker: interviewer
text: Knowledgeable about what might happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was like-- believe in God. She believed in God. And she said that he's such a-- minute she said it, God should help. One child should survive. And I survived. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for doing this interview this afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. You're welcome. How long will take you, you going to make--
